desc:
From here the track continues to the south and east, while towards the west the
moorland stretches out before you. Deep ruts in the track suggest that it has
been poorly maintained, but since the nearby cities have long since been
destroyed this is not surprising. The faint howls of wolves can be heard further
westwards, upon the moors. Past the flow of the Sirannon, to the south, a range
of small hills can be seen. The sight of these hills should bring forth old and
forgotten tales of the tribes of fierce hillmen that once lived upon these
lands.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
You are traveling upon the seemingly endless track that was once, very long ago,
used by dwarves and elves when they carried goods between Moria and
Ost-in-Edhil. Today, the track is no longer used by dwarves and elves in their
search of trade, for the areas along this path are no longer inhabited. To the
north, some hills can be seen while to the south what remains of the once mighty
river Sirannon can be heard.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The old road continues east and west, as it provides a path between the Misty
Mountains and Tharbad. Wind-blown leaves, holly berries, and rugged stones
blanket the road as if they are trying to hide the lack of usage of the barren
tracks. To the south, beyond the river Sirannon, the Hollin Hills can be seen,
while, to the north, more hills hover on the horizon.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The old road, once favoured by the wagons of long dead rich merchants, con-
tinues to the East and West. To the south, the streambed of the Sirannon runs
quietly and solemnly parallel to the road. All sorts of different types of
terrain surrounds the area. Grassy hills and bleak forests hugs the road to the
north.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
Great rolling plains of tall green grass open up.. The grass is very lush and
green, a welcome change for your feet as well. Horses could easily graze here.
The side of the road seems to be reachable to the east.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
The track continues east and south here through the countryside. To your north
the wood continues and you can enter it from here. The track is hot and dusty,
and the cool shade of the wood seems very inviting.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The road bends to the north here and you can see the river bank to the east.
Looking east you can see the Sirannon winding its way up towards Caradhras and
the Misty Mountains. You can see many prints that have been set in the mud
before you travelled along this track.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
This is a peaceful woodland, and all around the wood's population is at work or
play. The sun glistens through the leaves and casts friendly shadows on the
ground. From here one can see the track to the south and the wood continues
north and east.
name: Quiet Woods

desc:
This is a glade with a small pool in the centre of it. Around the pool there are
many of the woodland creatures quietly drinking its refreshing waters.
Everything seems so peaceful here, but scorch marks on the trunks of some of the
older trees lets you know that it was not always like this. The wood continues
east and south.
name: Quiet Woods

desc:
The wood is probably at its thickest here, but there are still small clearings
here and there. Looking into them you can see some deer quietly grazing. There
are many tracks of animals on the ground but none really to raise alarm. The
wood continues to the east, south, and west.
name: Quiet Woods

desc:
The wood thickens slightly here making progress more difficult. The birds are
still singing in the trees and animals still play around you. Even though the
animals seem friendly, they always seem to keep a distance, never letting you
get too close. The wood continues north and west.
name: Quiet Woods

desc:
The track bends east and south here as it follows the path of the riverbed of
the old Sirannon. Looking to the east you can see the mountains rising up, but
those are many leagues away from here and of no concern at the moment. The track
is criss-crossed with many footprints and tracks of animals.
name: Bend in Hollin Road

desc:
This is a thinly populated wood. Still, there are many small animals scurrying
around your feet. The trees here are mainly deciduous. The wood continues to the
west and the decaying road to the east.
name: Quiet Woods

desc:
You enter the remains of what must have been the town's watchtower. From here
you can see a short way along the track. There are a couple of broken chairs and
tables, but apart from that the room is completely bare, stripped by bandits
long ago. The only exit is to head back down the way you came.
name: A Ruined Watchtower

desc:
You enter a small guard house. Looking around the room you can see it was built
only for a couple of guards. When this area was used for trading that's all that
would have been needed as the temporary markets would have been heaving with
Dwarvern miners and Elven farmers. The Dwarves would have been enough to keep
the peace as they guarded their gems and ores very carefully. In the north-west
corner there is a flight of stairs leading up and there is an old gate back
outside to the east.
name: The Guard House

desc:
The wood is sparsly populated with small clearings here and many animals are
seen to be grazing in them. Generally the wood goes about its daily business
without a second thought to you, or so it would seem. The wood continues to the
east.
name: Quiet Woods

desc:
Looking at the ground here there seems to be a great many tracks arriving from
the north and leaving either east or west. Looking north you can see a gate
which is now an orange colour with the rust that has built up on it over the
centuries. Above you, you can see a watch tower that has seen better days. The
track disappears in the west and joins with a biggest road in the east.
name: A Track in a Clear Forest

desc:
The old road continues east and west following the path of the Sirannon. There
is a small wood some distance away. The wind picks up and blows the dust around
you making you cough and splutter. To the west you can see the road winding its
way down into the valley.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The road continues east and west following the riverbed of the Sirannon. There
is a small wood some distance off to the north. The wind picks up and blows the
dust around you making you cough and splutter. To the west you can see the old
road winding its way into the valley.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The bed of the river is not what it used to be. All that is left is a stream not
nearly as wide as the original Sirannon. This river was much bigger as it is
now, but some climatics modifications, perhaps, have changed the facts. The
stream floods east-west.
name: Streambed of the Sirannon

desc:
The small stream flooding in a bed too large for him, residue of its past
importance, bends here. It is flooding from the east, as far as you can see, but
will, from here, floods southward. A small decaying road follows the bed of the
Sirannon to the north and the west.
name: Streambed of the Sirannon

desc:
The riverbed continues north and south from this point. A small stream runs
through the centre of the riverbed, over a layer of smooth round pebbles. The
walls of the riverbed are wrinkled with dry cracks and fissures, due to the lack
of water which once formed roaring Sirannon. An occasional weed protrudes from
the muddy cracks, determined to take root in all but the most unlikely places.
name: Streambed of the Sirannon

desc:
Here the riverbed curves to the north and west, around the base of a small
berry-covered hill. A beaver dam has been built through the great riverbed, to
regulate the level of the water during the seasons in the pool it created. The
top of a large beaver hut emerges from the water. To the east and south, more
holly-covered hills are visible beyond the riverbed, splashing the landscape
with a flurry of pinkish hue.
name: Dam in the Sirannon

desc:
A lonely hill stands here, overlooking the Sirannon streambed to the east and
south. Low hollyberry bushes cover the ground in abundance, laden with heavy
bunches of ripe hollyberries. A shabby old road can be seen to the west while
far to the east, the towering Misty Mountains loom gracefully with the three
giant snow-covered peaks.
name: Lonely Hill

desc:
The Sirannon flows from east to west here, occupying a small portion of its
ancient streambed. The water is cold and clear, and a reasonable effort is
needed to hold position in the low but swift stream as it gathers pace, fed from
the surrounding hills. Reeds and other marsh plants have colonised the wet
riverbed, while the slopes are covered by grass. Far to the east, behind several
lush hills, the towering Misty Mountains can be seen.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
The low river flows swiftly from east to west from here. The water is crystal
clear and very cold, coming directly from the different tributaries which flow
through the hills. It is difficult to navigate the river as the current drags
you towards the west, making for a very bumpy ride. To the south the green
hills, which are dotted red by berry laden holly bushes, can be safely reached
from here. To the north you can see the Hollin Road.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
The river Sirannon flows swiftly here, from east to west. The water is clear and
cold, being composed of melting snow from the nearby mountains. The current is
rather strong and carries debris which has collected along the river's route. To
the south, hills dotted with red, by hovering holly plants, seem safe and
beckoning.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
The river leads directly to the of south here, the water moving with
considerable speed as it flows over the pebbles which litter the riverbed. A
broad track, that runs across the northern areas of this land, can be reached by
climbing the riverbank to the north or west. In the south, a few small hills can
be seen. The water here is clear and bright, and several species of aquatic life
be seen.
name: Bend in the Sirannon

desc:
The river flows from the north, around a small bend, the channel then forces the
water to the west, bending once again further on. A spray of water hits you in
the face as it flies upwards from a nearby rock. To the south of you the
wetlands, typical of this region, can be seen covered with foliage such as holly
bushes and heather. There is also a track paralleling the river on its northern
bank, that looks well used.
name: River Sirannon

desc:
The Sirannon continues its path through the Hollin Hills from east to west here.
The remnants of the once-mighty river lie across the entire width of the flat
riverbed, allowing you to cross here with ease. To the west, however, the river
gathers strength and pace as it is fed by rivulets and tributaries from the
pink-red hills which pervade this area.
name: Ford in the Sirannon

desc:
A well used trail winds its way through the foothills, leading from a ford in
the Sirannon just north, to the south will it continues through a thin forest.
To the east and west, the plains begin to rise as they form the Hollin hills. In
the distant east, the crowns of the Misty Mountains can barely be seen above the
hill-tops.
name: Trail among the Plains

desc:
The trail travels through a sparse forest here. Many deciduous trees grow here,
interspersed with some smaller holly bushes. The trail itself continues from
north to south, leading out of the forest into the hills to the south. The trail
does not appear to be very busy, but it seems to have been used lately.
name: Trail in Light Forest

desc:
The trail bends from east to north here as it travels through the pink-red hued
hills. The hills lie in all directions, but a small forest intercedes between
here and the hills to the north and east. To the south, the holly bushes thin
out and grass becomes more predominate, although an occasional bush can still be
seen.
name: Trail in the Hills

desc:
The trail travels from east to west of here, journeying through as small forest
as it does so. To the west, the trail enters some hills. The forest continues to
the north and north-west, but the southernmost point of it lies here. The forest
is sparse and seems lucky to have manged to exist in this area.
name: Trail in Light Forest

desc:
Here, the trail bends from west to south, avoiding the grassy plain which extend
east. To the west, beyond some low hills, the beginning of a forested valley is
faintly visible. The trail is evidently used often, apparently by men, or
man-like beings. To the north, the Hollin Hills stretch out for miles.
name: Well-worn Trail

desc:
The trail comes to a sudden end where it meets a crudely made palisade gate.
Blackened tree trunks extend sharpened ends well above the ground, hiding and
shielding from view whatever might be stirring inside. Several skulls have been
fixed on the palisade as warning for the foreigners. The fields surrounding the
palisade are covered with cow dung and show obvious signs of grazing -- whoever
dwells behide the palisade surely depends on herds for their sustenance. The
trail continues north through the grasslands, giving way to a sparse growth of
trees.
name: In Front of a Palisade

desc:
These grasslands are fertile, and obviously well-grazed. The palisade visible to
the southeast seems to indicate that whatever grazes here is probably
domesticated. To the north, the low hills begin to show some woody growth, while
the grassy hills to the west become slightly more rugged. To the east is a trail
leading up to the front gate of the walled village.
name: Grasslands

desc:
This area of low rolling hills is carpeted with grass -- parts obviously well-
grazed by some sort of herd animal. To the east, a sturdy palisade of logs forms
a barrier, which, one might assume, protects the nearby villagers who use these
grasslands for grazing live stock.
name: Grasslands beside a Palisade

desc:
The grassy hills have been closely cropped by grazing. To the west, an imposing
log palisade rears up among the low hills - probably protecting a village. To
the south, the hills continue, hiding the course of the River Glanduin somewhere
among them. To the east, the grasslands continue unabated.
name: Grasslands beside a Palisade

desc:
To the southwest, a log palisade is visible among the low grass-covered hills.
The landscape looks heavily grazed - perhaps the people on the other side of the
wall depend on cattle. To the west, a well-worn trail leads to what looks like a
gate in the palisade.
name: Outskirts of a Village

desc:
These low hills are carpeted with a tough grass that helps them resist erosion.
To the east, the landscape becomes much flatter, and there is a village of some
sort. In every other direction the hills continue - becoming more forested to
the northwest.
name: Sloping Grassland

desc:
These lands were apparently once much more forested than now. In the small
valleys tucked between the high points, only stumps remain. There hasn't been
much regrowth, so the trees were probably cut fairly recently. To the west,
woods again become visible among the hills.
name: Grassland of Eregion

desc:
Nestled among the surrounding hillsides, this small well-watered valley is the
home to a dense but young forest. You can tell animal life is plentiful for
there are signs of small mammals everywhere. To the north, the hills are much
more rugged while in the other directions they are characterized by gentle
slopes.
name: Wooded Valley

desc:
This terrain is dominated by gentle well-worn hills and lightly wooded vales. To
the east there is a valley filled by what could almost be described as a forest.
Holly bushes are not as prevalent here as in Hollin to the northeast, but it
still lingers.
name: Hills of Eregion

desc:
To the northwest you can see a pile of stones which must be near the bank of the
river Sirannon. The ground, further north of here, seems to get very wet and may
not be able to support your weight. The moors to the south of here are lightly
wooded by stunted trees which strive to survive the battering winds.
name: Northern Dunland Moors

desc:
While the moor is quite dry here a few heather plants, which cover the ground,
seem to be growing very well. Scruff marks and a series of small holes on the
plants suggest that wildlife around here is not as dead as in the surrounding
moorlands. You are a long ways from the river, which lies to the north, and
there are signs of human habitation to the west, as you can just make out a
building in the distance.
name: Gentle Hills

desc:
The hills extend in all directions from here. To the east, they are somewhat
more rugged, but do not form much of an obstacle. To the north, the faint
glimmer of what must be the river Sirannon can be seen. Somewhere among the
hills to the south must lie the river Glanduin, and beyond that an expanse of
land known as the Dunland.
name: Hills of Eregion

desc:
These hills are particularly rugged for this region - the land looking as if it
had been pushed back and folded unto itself. Eastward the hills begin to fail,
while to the south and west they continue unchanged. Some distance to the north,
the river Sirannon can be spotted winding along the northern edge of the hills.
name: Folded Hills

desc:
The hills become much more rugged to the west, while to the east the hills seem
to fade somewhat. Mostly covered with wiry grass, these hills are well-worn by
the passage of time. To the southwest, it appears that some small areas of
forest have managed to thrive in the little valleys.
name: Low Hills

desc:
This appears to be the eastern edge of an expanse of hilly land. To the east,
the land flattens significantly, becoming essentially a plain. The grassy
hillsides appear well-grazed, so there must be a human habitation near. To the
north, a trail winds among the shallow depressions between the high points of
the land.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
The pink-red hills stretch out far to the west, holly bushes dominating the
landscape. Few plants have been able to survive on these harsh hills. A trail
can be seen, travelling through the hills and continuing into a forest
northward. Several miles behind this forest, you can see a river flanked by an
overgrown road.
name: Hills of Eregion

desc:
All around you terrain appears to be hills, covered by holly bushes, that
stretch for miles and miles. Far to the north you can see an overgrown road
hugging the northern bank of a narrow river as they travel together from east to
west. To the east, the hills are punctuated by a small forest as they lead to
the foothills of the Misty Mountains, many miles from here.
name: Hills of Eregion

desc:
The hills continue in every direction - somewhat steeper to the east - and are
covered with the growths of holly bushes that are common in this region. To the
north, the Sirannon winds among the hills leading westward to its junction with
the Glanduin.
name: Hills of Eregion

desc:
Although you are a long ways south of the river Sirannon, you can just make out
the steep banks through the overhanging tree branches in this area of woodland.
Your sight to the northwest is completely blocked by the overgrown trees and
bushes, but off to the southwest you can see some sort of structures built on a
small hill.
name: Wooded Hills

desc:
The moorland, sloping gently down to the nearby river, continues to the west,
where in the far distance there are a few signs of civilization. The vegetation
here is hardy, able to withstand the rocky soil of the hills, as well as the
harsh weather that comes frequently. A sharp howl of a wolf makes you realise
you are truly far from home.
name: Rolling Moorland

desc:
The hills, dotted with berry laden holly bushes, stretch in all directions,
painting the landscape with their red berries. To the north, you can barely make
out a river which winds its way across the countryside as a road hugs the
northern bank. To the south-east, you can see a forest in the distance and far,
far in the distant east the Misty Mountains can be seen as they reach towards
the sky.
name: Hills of Eregion

desc:
The hills, covered with berry laden holly bushes, continue here, as they do in
all directions. To the east, while the hills become smaller, the holly bushes do
not thin and the ever present red berries continue to cover the bushes. To the
distant north, you can see a road which follows the route of a river as they
wind their way through the countryside.
name: Hills of Eregion

desc:
Small hills lie in the shadow of their larger counterparts to the west. In any
case, your view to the north, west and south is blocked. To the east, however,
you can see a forest. Far beyond the forest, the Misty Mountains can still be
seen, towering above all else.
name: Low Hills

desc:
Holly bushes litter the countryside, their rich pink-red berries adding color to
the region. Dots of pink-red continue far to the east, spreading also to the
south-east. The berries are also visible, but for a shorter distance, to the
west. The Sirannon flows through its wide riverbed to the north, skirted by the
Hollin Road to its north. To the south, the land begin to transform as the
pink-red colour of berries is replaced by lush green. The Misty Mountains caress
the sky, dominating the horizon far to the east.
name: Sirannon Plain

desc:
The Hollin Hills stretch south of here, flanked to the north by the remains of
the river Sirannon and dozens of miles south by the mighty Glanduin. Beyond and
adjacent to the river Sirannon, the Hollin Road winds its way east-west, joining
the once great settlements of Moria and Ost-in-Edhil. Even from here, you can
see that the road has suffered from a lack of use. Obviously, not many in
current times have need of the road. The holly bushes of this region, as always,
cling to the side of the hills adding a touch of read to the lush green
background with their many berries.
name: Sirannon Plain

desc:
From this vantage point over the river, you can see, clearly, the southern
hills. They are all covered by red dots which cling to the holly bushes growing
upon them. To the north, the river Sirannon picks up speed and power, as it is
fed by small rivulets and tributaries from this watered land. Clinging to the
north bank of the river lies the Hollin Road, overgrown due to lack of use. Far
to the east, beyond the holly-covered hills, are the Misty Mountains, visible
even from this distance.
name: Sirannon Plain

desc:
While the majority of the land here is flat, the ground slopes upwards to the
north, forming a hill. The ground is covered with a mixture of hay and tall
grasses, growing to waist height. While no flowers can compete for sunlight
against this tall vegetation, goldenrod grows in profusion, showering the area
with a shimmering layer of yellow.
name: Tall Grass

desc:
This low and long hill runs from the east to the west, creating a large ridge in
the plains. The lower slopes are covered by hay, which thins out as the hill
rises. The remainder of this knoll has a green layer of grass across its entire
length, with patches of clover growing in places. This raised vantage allows for
a fairly good view of the surrounding land.
name: Small Grassy Rise

desc:
This area is completely covered by tall hay which rises more than waist-high.
There is an occasional sign of animal life, from meandering paths in the
vegetation to droppings. Despite the thriving growth, many rocks litter the
ground. A slope rises above the plains to the south, getting progressively more
steep.
name: Fields beside a Small Hill

desc:
A high layer of hay and wild grains covers the ground, which swells slightly at
intermittent intervals. The dips in the ground shelter clusters of bushes, which
grow huddled together. There are a series of small holes dug into the ground,
with little mounds of excavated dirt marking their openings.
name: Green Fields

desc:
Some bushes dot the area to the north, thinning out and stopping as the ground
becomes level. A thin strip of grass lies between the end of the bushes and the
wild hay that grows to the chest height of a man. This area seems to be seldom
visited by animals, for the hay is strangely undisturbed. To the west, down a
steep bank, flows a clear river.
name: Grassy Field

desc:
The Riverbed leads to the north and west here, seeming to become slightly more
clear as if some fresh source of water added to its flow from somewhere to the
north. The clearer flow still contains little life though as it continues its
slow movement across the land. You may climb onto the riverbank to the north or
follow the bed to the east or west.
name: Streambed of the Sirannon

desc:
As you move along the riverbed of the once mighty Sirannon all your cares and
worries just seem to vanish. The stream flowing north and west is very
comforting and you may follow it in both directions.
name: Streambed of the Sirannon

desc:
This place on the riverbank of the once proud river Sirannon is very tranquil
indeed. There are birds in the trees, and the insects enjoying the muddy waters
to the north. The riverbed lies to the north and the riverbank continues to the
west.
name: Clear River

desc:
The banks of the riverbed move apart here where the flow of the river must once
have been very wide. There is not much movement below the surface of the small
stream the river has become now, the lack of water causing most of the
waterbourne life to die or move long ago. The river continues east and south.
name: Streambed of the Sirannon

desc:
A small breeze keeps the smell of the muddy riverbed away from you here. The
riverbed leads east and west. A small tributary comes from the south, joining
forces with the once very mighty Sirannon.
name: Streambed of the Sirannon

desc:
Still the river twists and turns, having shaped the surrounding land to it's
will. This particular bend in the river is to the west and north, and is still
bordered by hills to the south, which are not quite accessible from here. To the
north-west is an ancient track, and it seems that the streambed is shallow
enough that you can clamber up onto it as well.
name: Streambed of the Sirannon

desc:
The clear river, cutting through the Hollin Hills, brings more water to the
sandy riverbed north. The water, flowing from the sprinkled hills to the south,
is a trickle of clear water running down the middle of this riverbed. To the
south, the Hollin hills can be reached and to the west, some faint animal tracks
can be seen. Far in the east, the Misty Mountains can be seen with their
menacing snow-covered peaks.
name: Clear River

desc:
The river flows north and south, coming from the luxuriant forested hills to the
east. The clear water mirrors the various lush vegetation growing along the
riverbanks. To the south and west, peculiar-looking, red dotted hills can be
seen. Far to the east, the Misty Mountains, which appears purple as they rise to
meet the sky in the distance, are capped by glistening white.
name: Clear River

desc:
The river flows north, coming from the luxuriant forested hills east. The clear
water sprays a lush vegetation growing on the banks. To the south and west,
peculiar-looking, hills can be seen, while to the far east, the Misty Mountains
can be seen, with their awe-inspiring peaks looming above the land.
name: Clear River

desc:
In this bed the Sirannon continues on its neverending trek to find the sea. The
land does not seem to have suffered at all, still looking well-watered. From
here it is possible to continue along the meandering streambed. To the north
seems to be an old overgrown road that might be worth exploring.
name: Streambed of the Sirannon

desc:
The streambed makes a slow bend here, continuing to the north and west. The
landscape surrounding the waterway is quite breathtaking, with the lush hills
and forest to the north, and the majesty of the snow-capped mountains looming
far in the east. Dwarf and Elf alike chose their homes of old well indeed.
name: Streambed of the Sirannon

desc:
Here the shallow bed makes yet another bend, this time to the south and east.
There is lush greenery all around, particularly to the north of the river, where
lie hills adorned with wildflowers of various shapes, sizes, and colours. Even
the slightly more desolate lands visible to the south have more than their share
of the vibrant Hollinish flora.
name: Streambed of the Sirannon

desc:
What a picturesque scene this must have been before the stream was slowed down,
with the vibrant colours and the mountains in the hazy distance. Even now that
the Sirannon has decreased its flow, the landscape is still lovely indeed. It is
no wonder the Elves of ages past chose this to be their home on this side of the
sea.
name: Streambed of the Sirannon

desc:
On and on the shallow bed continues. All of the flora greets your eye with
vibrant colour, except for a few dead plants. Up to the north is an ancient
track, and is seems that the streambed is shallow enough that you clamber onto
it.
name: Streambed of the Sirannon

desc:
The land here its in a slight depression, seeming even lower because of the high
ridge to the east. The soil is thick and slightly damp, due to the river running
to the west of here. Patches of moss and weeds grow between the heavy layers of
bushes that abound here. There is a definite slope to the east, which rises to
form a high ridge. The land rises gently to the north and south to become level
with the plains.
name: Meadow beside the Ridge

desc:
This land rests in a small hollow, shaped by a ridge to the east and a vertical
wall of rock to the north. Small coniferous trees dot the area, mostly by the
cliff-face in the north. These trees give way to smaller bushes and shrubs to
the south. Little grows under the heavy layer of needles underneath the trees,
but a constant and green layer of grass covers the rest of the area.
name: Forested Meadow

desc:
You walk easily on a wide track, which runs alongside the dried Sirannon. This
once may have been a well-kept and well-travelled thoroughfare, but since the
fall of Ost-in-Edhil it has become somewhat overgrown with low grasses. You
can't help but smile as you look at your peaceful surroundings, with the shallow
streambed continuing to the south of you, and some rugged hills further north.
As you look to the east your eyes linger for a moment on the mighty mountains of
Moria: Caradhras, Celebdil, and Fanuidhol.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The track bends here, seemingly at the mercy of the streambed winding to the
east and south. The local flora here is lush and well-watered, thanks to the
generous rainfall this area must recieve. Even the rugged hills to the north are
not lacking in foliage of vibrant green and various wildflowers of all colours
and hues. You see also a number of animals, but nothing you immediately discern
as being threatening to your presence here. To the north your way is blocked by
some bushes overgrown with thick holly and thistles.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The old road continues south and east following the path of the dried-up
Sirannon. There is some small hills just north of here. The wind picks up blows
the dust around you making you cough and splutter. To the west you can see the
road winding its way down into the valley.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The road here is quite dusty and the wind continually picks up the dust and
whips it around you, making you cough and splutter. There are some hills to the
north with the path leading off towards them.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
Looking north from here you can see a path leading off alongside the stream. The
track still continues east and west and seems to be used a bit more on this side
as there are many tracks leading in both directions.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The road bends east and south here as it follows the banks of the riverbed of
the Sirannon. Looking to the east you can see the mountains rising up, but those
are many leagues away at the moment. The road is crisscrossed with many
footprints and tracks of animals. A small track enters the woods to the west.
name: Bend in the Road

desc:
The road bends to the north here and you can see the river bank to the east.
Looking east you can see the bed of the old Sirannon river winding its way up
towards Caradhras and the Misty Mountains. You can see many prints that have
been set in the mud before you travelled along this track.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
The hedgerow is quite dense here and the animals cross your path contimually.
You can see the track to the north, and plains open up to your east. Looking at
the ground, you can see many tracks in the dirt. There seems to be an old
tributary of the Sirannon here.
name: A Hedgerow

desc:
The river must once have been much wider here but now the water flows as three
different narrow streams, all being easily crossed by foot. This must once have
been a very peaceful stretch of the river, but now the mud and decomposing
insects make the smell almost unbearable. The riverbed continues east and west.
name: Riverbed of the Sirannon

desc:
The road continues as far as you can see, running parallel to the riverbed of
the Sirannon. There is a vibracy and life in the air here, heightened by the
presence of the local wildlife, and the singing of the many types of birds that
inhabit the land. However you realise that you are not terribly far now from
Moria, the once great dwarven home of Khazad-dum. Indeed, the shadows of
Caradhras, Celebdil, and Fanuidhol already lie across these lands, dampening the
carefree feeling in the air, and making the atmosphere more somber.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
You are on an old track that was once a major thoroughfare for travellers to and
from Ost-in-Edhil, the achitectural wonder built by the Gwaith- i-Mirdain. The
road has since fallen into somewhat of a state of disrepair, and is overgrown in
several spots. The track runs on the north side of the Sirannon, and to the
south of the riverbed you see the Dunlandish wilderness. The mountains of Moria,
known to humans as Redhorn, Silvertine, and Cloudyhead, dominate the landscape
to the east, towering above you even though they are still quite a distance off.
name: Hollin Road

desc:
These green hills start to rise up around you and you can see them rising higher
to the north. Looking south you can see the small hills as they head down
towards the road. The grass is very lush here and was probably used for grazing
some time ago. You can follow the hills to the north and south, or go onto a
path to the west.
name: Foothills

desc:
You are well into the hills now, and on either side the lush, green hills rise
above you. To the south you can see the road and the riverbed of the Sirannon
winding its way through this region. The air is fresh and clean, but you seem to
sense an uneasiness in the land itself, as if it is still not at peace with
itself. You can go north and south through the hills, or west onto a path.
name: Foothills

desc:
The hills begin to rise quite steeply around you here. To the east is a small
cabin with smoke rising in a column from the chimney. There are footprints
leading in and out of the cabin suggesting that it is used quite frequently. The
air is clean, the grass green, and all is at peace. The exits are east, north,
and south.
name: Green Hills

desc:
This is a small, well kept cabin used by the areas hunters. In the centre of the
room there is a table with chairs sitting around it. Along the walls are shelves
stocked with provisions for those who did not hunt well and have returned empty
handed. A fireplace dominates the eastern wall.
name: A Hunter's Cabin

desc:
The hills in this country are very quiet. Most of them are covered with dense
grass. The others are covered by a clear wood. The area is very quiet and should
be a good hunting area.
name: Green Hills

desc:
The hills rise up all around you, and you notice a small path leading west.
Looking along this path as it twists its way round the hills, you notice that
there are very few tracks of any kind on it. You are quite content as the lush
grass is soft underfoot. You can go south and north.
name: Green Hills

desc:
The grass here is very green and very juicy looking. There are some animals
grazing around you, and you feel completely at ease. Then again you should
always be on the lookout for trouble as it could lie round every bend. The hills
continue south and west.
name: Green Hills

desc:
The hills rise up all around you, and you notice a small path leading west.
Looking along this path as it twists its way round the hills, you notice that
there are very few tracks of any kind on it. You are quite content as the lush
grass is soft underfoot. You can go east and west.
name: Green Hills

desc:
The hills rise up all around you, and you notice a path leading west. Looking
along this path as it twists its way around the hills, you notice that there are
very few tracks of any kind on it. You are quite content as the lush grass is
soft underfoot.
name: Green Hills

desc:
The hills still rise up around you, but the path has become more defined and
begins to head down a gentle slope into a valley. The tracks on the path have
become more numerous and it seems to be leading to a watering hole of some kind.
You can go south and east.
name: Green Hills

desc:
You follow this path down, and the further you go the steeper it gets. Small
streams trickle down beside you and the sound of falling water can be heard
ahead of you, although not very loud over the sound of the wind. There are many
other tracks on the path, but they are unrecognisable as they are all one on top
of the other. You can go east and north.
name: Path Leading Down

desc:
You enter the remains of a farmhouse which by the looks of things was destroyed
long ago. Only a few walls remain along with the chimney stack. Looking around
you find only the odd remnant of furniture, a carved table and chairs along with
a few pieces of cutlery. The only exit is back to the west.
name: Ruined Farmhouse

desc:
A long time ago this field must have been used for grazing, but looking at the
dead clumps of grass you can see this is not so now. There is an air of
emptiness surrounding you and you feel very open and vulnerable. Looking to the
east you can see a ruined farmhouse, and the foothills rise up to the south.
name: Desolate Fields

desc:
The hills rise up all around you, and you soon begin to lose your sense of
direction. The lush grass that grows around your feet is criss-crossed by many
small trails. On examining these trails you see that small trails don't always
mean small creatures as there are some rather large footprints in the mud.
Caution may be best advised here until you can work out where you are.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
The green grass grows all around you. In and amongst this greenary you notice
several large droppings and begin to wonder what could have made anything that
large. The area seems very naturally lush here though, and little danger is
apparent.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
The hills are smaller here and the grass all around has a trampled look to it.
There are some footprints leading to the north but then they just seem to
disappear. Some of the grass has been cut up and there are bits of turf and sod
lying all around. The hills continue east and south.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
The hills rise up very steeply to the south and west blocking all passage in
those directions. The lush grass that grows all around has some large footprints
in it. The hills continue east and north.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
The hills rise up all around you, and you soon begin to lose your sense of
direction. The lush grass that grows around your feet is criss-crossed by many
small trails. On examining these trails you see that small trails don't always
mean small creatures as there are some rather large footprints in the mud.
Caution may be best advised here until you can work out where you are.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
The hills begin to rise up and your path has many animal tracks crossing it, as
well as some very large footprints. You begin to lose your sense of direction
but can still see a few buildings to your east. The ground tremors slightly in
what seems to be all directions. The hills continue north and west.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
This is the western end of a small range of low hills lying to the north of an
area of open plains. The hill range rises up higher to the north, into an area
of rolling hills where the vegetation seems to be more abundant than the low
shrubs on this hillside.
name: The Lowhills

desc:
This area of open plain is bounded by thick bushes to the west and south. A low
range of hills rise up to the north and continue eastwards, but the ground to
the immediate east is much flatter. The vegetation in this area probably
contains plenty of wildlife that could provide a skilled hunter with some to
eat.
name: Open Plains

desc:
This piece of open ground before the old wooden gate may once have been a
cultivated field or part of a rough marketplace set up beside the river. A dock
of some kind lies to the east and and small tower has been built to the west.
Looking northwards you can see the ground slowly rising up into some rolling
hills in the distance.
name: Rough Field

desc:
You pick your way over the rotten planks that used to make up this jetty. It
certainly doesn't look safe and it would be a good idea to leave as soon as
possible. Looking around you can see the remains of a few boats and looking out
into the dried-up riverbed you can see the half-sunken remains of a barge,
buried in the silt. Somebody was leaving in a hurry and didn't make it.
name: A Disused Dock

desc:
The riverbanks widen here as they lead towards an area of the riverbed which
must once have been some type of ford. The smell of rotting vegetation is not
quite as strong now, but the swift flow of this stream must have once been much
more comforting than what it has become. To the west an old landing platform for
barges is perched on the riverbank, but you can't climb onto it from here.
name: Riverbed of a Tributary

desc:
It's along the riverbank that this path runs. Looking down at your feet you can
see many animal and human tracks. There are insects flying over the muddy
riverbed to the west and everything is at peace. The track is south from here
and the path continues north.
name: Path along the Stream

desc:
This gentle path rambles on northward with an area leading down to the dry
riverbed to the west. Around you the birds are singing and small animals scurry
here and there, mindless of your presence. To the east you can see some hills
rise up into the sky. You can go north and south along the path, with the
riverbed to the west, and some hills to the east.
name: Path along the Stream

desc:
This gentle path runs along parallel to the stream. Along the riverbank can be
seen the local wildlife going about their daily business. Everything seems to be
at peace here; there is little movement along the dried-up river now. The
desperate spawning attempts of the salmon long ago ceased on this stretch of
river. The path continues north and south.
name: Path along the Stream

desc:
The path arrives at an old building of a small size which seems to come out of
nowhere. The building itself seems to be in a very bad state of repair, with
many cracks in the walls. To the west the path drops down to a small landing
where a few small boats could have been tethered had the river not dried-up long
ago. The path continues to the south.
name: Path along the Stream

desc:
The wooden walls of this tavern are beginning to rot away leaving cracks for the
wind to howl through. The old casks that served this place smell of their foul
and rotted contents. A few old stools still stand by the bar, a simple thing
made of some tan coloured wood. You may go back through the door to the south or
north onto the deck.
name: An Abandoned Tavern

desc:
You enter a peaceful glade, and everything seems to be so calm - not even a
ripple on the pond before you. Huge old willows gently dip their branches into
the still waters. This must be the source of one of the streams that add to the
trickle that the once great river Sirannon has become. You can go west and north
to the hills, and south to the riverbed itself.
name: A Peaceful Glade

desc:
A trickle of water coming from the north makes the muddy stream slightly clearer
here, but it is still only a meagre flow compared to the mighty river that once
carried sail-barges. The teeming waterlife of the stream has also died, leaving
behind little but a muddy ravine in the lands.
name: Riverbed of a Tributary

desc:
The loss of the waters in this once fast-flowing river must have had an awful
impact on the surrounding peoples who would have relied on the supply of fish it
provided for food and income. The trickle of muddy water can do little to
support any life now, in fact the only movement you can see is that of mosquitos
breeding above the foetid waters.
name: Riverbed of a Tributary

desc:
The pathetic flow of the Sirannon's tributary continues along the riverbed
between the riverbanks here. There is no wildlife to see in the ravine at this
point, the fish having died long ago when the waters began to run low. The
riverbed leads to the north and south.
name: Riverbed of a Tributary

desc:
A breeze brings the foul smell of the rotting matter on the riverbed to your
nostrils as you wander along the damp riverbed. The decaying vegetation, brought
downstream from far away makes the stream take on a rusty-brown colour as it
continues it slow flow southwards.
name: Riverbed of a Tributary

desc:
This flat area is covered in rough grasses and small patches of low vegetation.
To the north the land begins to rise up into an area of more hilly terrain, and
to the east you can enter a dried-up riverbed of some kind. To the west the flat
ground continues, but southwest of you somewhere you can make out a short tower.
name: Rough Fields

desc:
The land becomes slightly hilly here, rising up from the flat ground to the
south and west, and towering above the dried-up riverbed of some stream to the
east. You may move onto the riverbank itself to the north, or between the banks
to the east. There is little wildlife apparent here, but the area is obviously
not devoid of life as some animal tracks of some kind cross this area of the
hills.
name: The Lowhills

desc:
This place seems to be very peaceful and you feel you could spend the rest of
your life here absorbing the tranquil atmosphere, but in the dark recesses of
your mind you feel the urge to press on. As you prepare to leave you wonder why
the once mighty river Sirannon and its tributary has now dried-up to become the
small trickle it is.
name: On the Bank of the Dried-up River

desc:
As you leave the lower ground to the south of you, the foothills begin to rise
up around you. The country is gently rolling and seems to be at peace as you
move higher. The grass grows up around your knees and gently sways in the
breeze. It must have been a long time after the Dark Lord laid waste to this
area, for the land has regenerated itself and is now calm. To the west you can
see the rolling hills rise up and lead off into the distance, to the north is an
abandoned farm, to the east you can see the river bank and to the south the
lower lying ground.
name: The Foothills

desc:
You are standing in the middle of a small range of low hills that rise up
further to the north. The hills also continue to the east and west, or you may
move onto the lower ground to the south. The sparse vegetation on this hillside
would not support many animals and there is little wildlife apparent here.
name: The Lowhills

desc:
This flat area of land is quite dry and would be very suitable to set up a place
to rest. The plain lies just to the south of some hills that rise up further to
the north. A dried-up stream is somewhere to the east of you, but more
noticeable is a short tower to the southwest.
name: Flat Plains

desc:
The smell down here is awful! Peering around in the gloom you can see that this
room must be the cellar of the ruined farmhouse, but no one seems to use it now.
A few broken barrels and casks lie here as well as the droppings of rats. The
only exit is back up.
name: A Musty Cellar

desc:
This small, shallow cave is quite dry and sheltered, but not enough to prevent
daylight to enter. Looking around, one notices some smoke marks on the walls
where fires have been. Against the northern wall is a large flat slab of stone.
The only exit seems to be south, but some etchings are on the wall, appearing to
be runes of some sort.
name: A Small Cave

desc:
This dark only sparsely lit by a few torches. The stone of the ground has been
worn smooth by thousands of feet walking over it. Looking at the walls one can
see that they have been painted and sculpted, but this must have been a long
time ago as the plaster is crumbling and the paint peeling. The only exit is to
the north.
name: A Dark Entryway

desc:
This is a dark hallway that is sparsely lit on either side by crude torches
attached to the pillars. Eastwards and westwards the hall disappear into the
darkness. This room was carved out of the rock with the great skill of the
Dwarves from past ages. Most of this skill has long been forgotten along with
this hallway as it would seem. The hallway runs north and south.
name: A Dark Hallway

desc:
The darkness here seems to be all consuming. You can hardly see past your own
nose let alone the other side of the room. As you examine the walls you begin to
realise how skilled the Dwarves really are. The walls are covered with cracked
murals of events long ago. The darkness is impenetrable but you can see the
hallway to the west.
name: A Dark Hall

desc:
The darkness here seems to be all consuming. You can hardly see past your own
nose let alone the other side of the room. As you examine the walls you begin to
realise how skilled the Dwarves really are. The walls are covered with cracked
murals of events long ago. The darkness is impenetrable but you can make out the
torches in the hallway to the east.
name: A Dark Hall

desc:
It is very dark here but you can still make out vague paintings on the walls as
you near the sides of the room. The paintings have been done by an elven hand,
because the artistry involved could never have been achieved by any Dwarf. You
can still make out torches to the east.
name: A Dark Hall

desc:
The hallway continues its way, sparsely lit on either side by torches. The halls
continues east and west disappearing off into the darkness. To the north a ring
of torches and a large circular room can be seen. The hallway continues north
and south.
name: A Dark Hallway

desc:
The walls of the hall seem to stretch on forever. All over the walls are
paintings depicting battles from some time in the past. The hall seems to
continue to the south and one can just make out a hallway to the east.
name: A Dark Hall

desc:
You arrive in a room with a large shaft drilled into the floor in front of you.
Set around this shaft is a steep spiral staircase. Following the staircase down
are torches hung from the wall. The murals continue depicting battles and other
occasions from the distant past. The exits are south and down.
name: Descending Stairs

desc:
A depression in the rocks has collected a fair amount of a cool, clear water.
Made possible by the constant seepage through the rock, the water seems to have
a constant source. It looks and smells clean, naturally filtered by the
sedimentary rock above. This must be where the Dwarves get their water needs
every day. You wonder how people can stay down here for so long, but this seems
to provide some explanation.
name: A Natural Pool

desc:
This seems to be the area where the dwarves who live in the mines congregate to
eat their small snacks. The boulders lying on the caverns floor are worn smooth
were they have been sat upon, and strange luminous slime covers parts of the
walls where they have fed on discarded crumbs. The smell of vegetation comes
from the south and to the east a cool damp breeze is felt.
name: Resting Place

desc:
The ground around you is covered in decaying vegetable matter that feeds the
growth of the small white mushrooms of various sizes. The warmth generated by
this decomposition keeps the chill of a breeze from the north to a minimum. The
poor miners who are forced to survive on such food might just kill intruders
just to steal their food for themselves.
name: Mushroom Patch

desc:
As you enter the blackness you have to keep one hand on the wall so as you don't
stray too far from something solid. The wall suddenly opens up to the east and
you can smell some sort of vegetation from that direction. A cool draft of air
suggests the caverns further to the east contain more water than the small pool
on the floor beside the rock-face here.
name: A Rough Damp Tunnel

desc:
The main thing here is the large round hole in the centre of the floor. Leaning
against one side of the wall is a stout wooden ladder. Down into the darkness
the base of the ladder is surrounded by lit torches. The ladder itself is
well-done, with some hardwood and seems to be well cared for.
name: A Ladder Going Down

desc:
The tunnel bends south and west here. To the south one can see another ring of
torches. The ground also slopes down towards the south. There are no paintings
on the walls here and this tunnel looks newer than the rest, as the walls aren't
as smooth as the others.
name: A Mine Tunnel

desc:
This is obviously a treasure chamber. All around the walls take on a golden hue.
The large chamber contains little wealth now but empty chests line the walls. A
faint glow issues from one of these which perhaps contains the remnants of the
dwarves' wealth. The only exit is back up into what looks like an operations
room.
name: The Treasury

desc:
The trail, bounded by the Hollin hills to the west and north and a river to the
east seems to lead in only one direction, south. The ground is covered with a
low growing foliage, mostly grass, and a few fallen twigs. The hills to the west
and north are covered with holly bushes while the river, to the east, is crystal
clear.
name: End of Animal Track

desc:
The trail, formed by animal tracks, continues in a northern-southern direction
from here. The trail is hard to discern, as its infrequent usage has allowed the
vegetation to grow freely over it. To the west are the Hollin hills, covered by
berry producing holly bushes, to the east is a small river.
name: Animal Track

desc:
Here, the tracks vanish under the lush vegetation, making it impossible to
follow the trail to the south. It seems that a much older trail, one used by
men, used to head eastwards, but has been engulfed by the nearby river when its
changed course. All around you, to the south and west, are the Hollin hills as
they stretch for miles. You can follow the trail to the north as it separates
the hills to the west and the river to the east.
name: Animal Track

desc:
The Hollin plain continue equally in all direction, and to the east a line of
trampled grass can be seen. Near to the north, the river Sirannon can be heard,
and just beyond the river, even from here, the remains of an ancient road can be
seen.
name: Hollin Plain

desc:
These plains, lush indeed, are veiled by an abundance of low growing but hardy
holly bushes. Only a moderate amount of rainfall is required for these thorny,
colourful plants to flourish. To the south-west, more of the ruddy hills of
Hollin continue. To the north, the Sirannon - by now a small river - makes its
way steadily toward the west.
name: Hollin Plain

desc:
The Hollin Plains flank the river Sirannon, as it bends from west to north. To
the south and east the grass extend, dotted by berry laden holly bushes. Far to
the east, the mysterious Misty Mountains can be seen, with three menacing
snow-covered peaks towering above the rest.
name: Sirannon Plain

desc:
This mound over the riverbed, although lower than the surrounding hills, gives
you a good view of the region. The hills are lush green and dotted red from
berries ripening on the holly bushes. You can hear the nearby river Sirannon and
to the west can gain access to the river. While the Sirannon is a small river,
once, many years ago, it was much larger. Now, it looks like a mere trickle in
the wide riverbed.
name: Sloping Grassland

desc:
The landscape of the low Hollin hills is one which incites peace and reflection.
This is one of the most fruitful regions of Middle-earth, and was for a while
the granary of the northern Numenorean kingdoms. Several plants growing here are
indeed cereals which returned to the wild after the last peasants left the
place.
name: Sloping Grassland

desc:
This land is a land of plains and of low and fertile green hills on which are
scattered saplings, young walnut trees and fruit trees. A line of dry grass and
hostile bushes grows over the remains of an enclosing wall. These hints, along
with a few blocks of stones worked by a capable hand, are enough to prove that
on this place a settling had been built.
name: Hollin Plain

desc:
From here, hills can be seen all around, dotted red by berries on the plentiful
holly bushes which pervade the area. Far to the east, you can see the crags of
the Misty Mountains as they reach towards the sky. Closer to hand, you can see
two rivers: One lies to the east and flows north to join the Sirannon as it
wends its way towards the west where it will join the Glanduin, many leagues
away.
name: Sloping Grassland

desc:
The holly bushes which dominate the countryside also dominate the colour of this
region, imposing their red berries and lush green leaves on the country- side.
The hills stretch for many miles in all directions. No end to the hills is
readily at sight, but you can see far in the east three rocky crowns of the
Misty Mountains.
name: Hollin Hills

desc:
The pink-red hills spread all around you, covered in holly bushes which seem to
have established a dominance in the area. Rolling hills stretch for miles in all
directions. To the north-west, the hills are punctuated by the river Sirannon
flowing softly between them and the Hollin Road which follows the path of the
river. Apart from the river, the horizon is entirely dominated by the hills,
except for the east, where the Misty Mountains reach towards the sky.
name: Hollin Hills

desc:
From the trail to the west, a gentle slope climbs the green side of the hill.
The low grass is slowly overcome by the holly-bushes which spread all around
you. The trail leads to a ford north-west of here and continues through a forest
far to the south, flanked by hills. To the north, you can easily make out the
forlorn Sirannon, a shadow of its former might, and beyond it, the Hollin Road.
The hills roll continuously to the east for many miles, and far, far beyond the
hills, the Misty Mountains can be seen, dominating the horizon.
name: Sloping Grassland

desc:
A small forest is here and continues to the south and west. To the north and
east, the land rises to form a range of hills, covered with holly bushes. Small
animals dart away at your coming and you can see other evidence of these animals
in the multitude of small tracks which criss-cross the forest.
name: Light Forest

desc:
The holly bushes cling to the hillside here as they do to the north and east. To
the west lies a small forest which continues to the south-west. A worn trail can
be seen to the south as it winds its way to a stockaded village farther to the
south.
name: Hollin Hills

desc:
The hills continue in all directions save south of here, outlined by the red
berries of the predominant flora, holly bushes. To the south, a faint trail
peters out, but continues to travel east. Beyond this trail, a village can be
seen just beyond some farmland.
name: Hollin Hills

desc:
The hills begin to flatten out as they merge into the Plains of Hollin to south.
To the north and west, however, the hills continue for many miles. Far in the
distant north, you can barely make out the Sirannon as it flows to the west,
skirted by the remnants of the Hollin Road. To the south-west, you can see the
faint outline of a small village which is on the outskirts of the nearby forest.
name: Sloping Grassland

desc:
The lush grass that grows around your feet is criss-crossed by many small
trails. On examining these trails you see that narrow trails don't always mean
small creatures as there are some rather large footprints in the mud. The trails
are partially covered by short, bushy vegetation, somewhat blending them with
the surroundings and making them hard to spot from a distance. To the west and
north the Hollin hills can be easily reached.
name: Large Hill

desc:
A natural culvert begins here, channeling the water draining from the slight
ridges to the east and south and forming a clear river flowing off to the north.
To the south and west some peculiar-looking hills can be seen rising from the
plains of Dunland, while far off on the eastern horizon the Misty Mountains
thrust up their awe-inspiring peaks to the sky.
name: A Natural Culvert

desc:
A mixture of grasses, both course and fine, grows underfoot, interspersed here
and there with wildflowers of varying colours. The occassional stalk of wheat
thrusting from the ground could indicate that this area was once cultivated, but
there are no other signs of recent habitation except for a faint wisp of smoke
to the southeast. Far off to the east the first peaks of the Misty Mountains
raise their grey shoulders to the sky.
name: Plains of Hollin

desc:
Growing among the high grasses are several different types of wild wheats,
indicating that this plain was once cultivated, in the faroff past. It would
appear that only animals live here, except for the smoke coming from the south.
The ground is hard to see, due to the unchecked vegetation growth.
name: Hollin Grassland

desc:
Animal tracks stretch in the high grasses, winding through the hills. To the
north, the hills are dotted red by berries of the predominate flora, holly
bushes. To the south, the hills are greener, as the bushes give way to grass.
name: Hollin Grassland

desc:
The sound of the local wildlife fills your ears and you stroll lazily along this
everlasting grassy plain, and you feel lost in a see of high grasses. To the
north, hills covered by holly bushes stretch for miles. To the south, you can
venture into what appears to be farmland and beyond it to a stockaded village.
Far to the east, the snow covered peaks of the Misty Mountains can be seen.
name: Hollin Grassland

desc:
The plains are marked by the red berries of holly bushes which characterise the
region. These plains look similar to the nearby hills, with the main difference
being that they are level. The plains continue east and south and a small
village can be seen in the distant west. Northwards, a faint trail, formed by
the animals of this region, is barely visible as it cuts through the hills.
name: Plains of Hollin

desc:
These hills are covered with a blanket of thin grass that clings tenaciously to
the earth as an effort to resist the strongs winds that scour this region. To
the east, the hills lessen and some plains extend for a distant. Far beyond the
plains, the grey peaks of the Misty Mountains can be discerned.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
Far to the east, the first peaks of the Misty Mountains stand like a grey stain
on the horizon. These plains continue unabated to the east until the foothills
are finally reached. At times, the terrain becomes less flat, and the rounded
hills are dotted by the holly that is characteristic of the area.
name: Plains of Hollin

desc:
This land of grassy plains and rolling hills continues unabated until the
foothills of the Misty Mountains are reached miles to the east. Somewhere among
the plains to the south, the river Glanduin flows, while somewhere else in the
distant north, the Sirannon follows the path of the old Hollin Road.
name: Plains of Hollin

desc:
In all directions, this land of low and grassy hills continues. The terrain is
somewhat flatter to the west, while the hills appear to get slightly more
pronounced to the north. In places, there is evidence that herd animals
occasionally graze here - perhaps a group of Dunlendings have crossed the river
Glanduin to make their home near.
name: Plains of Hollin

desc:
These low grass-covered hills roll unabated for miles in all directions.
Somewhere beyond the hills, to the south, flows the River Glanduin, and beyond
the river lies the Dunland. To the east, the grassy growth becomes dotted with
small patches of vivid green holly bushes. To the north, the hills are a bit
more rugged.
name: Grasslands

desc:
This small dark pathway lies just off the road to the south. It looks well
trampled, but the growth of new grass, and the encroachment of nearby bushes,
makes you unsure as to recent travel along it. No footprints can be easily seen,
but it has definitely been well-used at some point. Going west is made somewhat
difficult by a massive jumble of leaves, twigs, and other intertwining
brushwood. To the north lies more of this seemingly deserted path.
name: Shadowed Path

desc:
The ridges to the north and east converge at this spot, creating a wide area
some thirty feet above the plains. The slopes to the west and south are
generally moderate, while there is an almost vertical drop to the north-east.
Scant amounts of topsoil allow for only hardy weeds to grow on the rocky ground.
name: Tall Fold of Earth

desc:
Wild grasses cover every portion of the level ground, not showing any hint of
dirt or rock. Where the ground slopes up to the north, tougher weeds and bushes
abound, being more suited to that environment. The slope eventually rises to
become a high ridge, several times the height of a man.
name: South of a Tall Ridge

desc:
Thick and healthy grass grows in profusion, covering the land from horizon to
horizon with a solid carpet of green. Some portions of the land look as if they
have seen grazing in the recent past, with short-cropped grass and animal
droppings. Many of the patches of sheared grass are in the south, giving an
indication that something of interest to a herd may lie in that direction.
name: Lush Plains

desc:
A thick, green layer of grass covers the ground as far as the eye can see. Some
patches of wild flowers bloom, creating a colourful display. An animal track
winds its way generally north-south, making for an unseen destination. While it
would be marginally easier to follow the track, much more direct routes in
either direction would be to cut straight across the plains.
name: Lush Pasture Land

desc:
The ground is flat and covered with a constant swath of healthy grass. Some
portions of the vegetation have been eaten short by herbivores, but most are
still growing wild and untamed. The occasional patch of flowers does little to
change the overall appearance of the place. It is a vast open area with little
cover and less variety.
name: Vast Prairie

desc:
The land here is composed of a series of slopes and the ground is covered with a
healthy layer of grass. There seems to be a thin but good layer of topsoil for
the grass is flourishing thick and green upon the hard ground. The lack of
bushes and flowers in this area make it somewhat dull and monotonous.
name: In the Prairie

desc:
A series of swells in the ground form a large number of minuscule hills. The
ground is covered in a lush and healthy layer of vegetation. Foot-high grass
makes up the majority of the greenery, while many different forms of grains rise
up in patches much too small to be harvested. The swells seem to grow in number
to the east, and become longer and more gentle to the north.
name: Lush Grass

desc:
The ground here is almost perfectly flat, with rare swells and dips which do
little to change elevation or impede vision. A rich and healthy layer of grass
covers the ground, adding a spring to the step. Small shrubs can be seen
clustered together, making low windbreaks. There are signs that animals often
come to this area to graze or eat berries off of the shrubs.
name: Lush Grass

desc:
Long grasses rise out of this mostly flat landscape, with patches of hay and
other wild grains vying for the sun. Animals have made many paths in the long
grass, seeming to go nowhere in particular. There is a low and grassy hill to
the north that breaks up the seeming monotony of this landscape.
name: Tall Grass in the Plains

desc:
The land here ascends to a long, thin hill which runs from the east to the west.
Its entire length is covered in a thick and rich layer of grass, with several
small patches of flowers providing some colour and scent. The hill descends
steeply to the north and south, but has a gentle slope which gradually merges
into the plains to the east.
name: Raised Ground

desc:
While the vegetation covering the ground here is over a foot tall, it's made up
of weeds and grass rather than any domesticated sort of grain. The ground has
some slope to it, which seems to rise as it travels to the west, and descend to
the east. There is little colour to the scenery, with few flowers able to push
up past the grass towards the sun.
name: Fields by a Hill

desc:
The ground here appears to be fashioned in the shape of a very wide and fairly
shallow bowl. The mostly flat sides of the incline make for easy surveying, but
since the viewer is standing in the middle of an approximately ten-foot
depression, the line-of-sight is limited. The ground vegetation is predominately
a constant layer of grass, intermixed with patches of clover and small
wildflowers.
name: Wide Plains

desc:
A layer of grass covers the ground, sparse in places. Stones and bare earth
create a series of brown patches, overshadowed by many small shrubs which lie
close to the ground. Piles of dung litter the area, mostly clustered around
patches of grass which have been eaten close to the ground. There are many small
and aimless animal tracks in the area, but one particularly well-trodden appears
to lead to the east.
name: West of a Small Camp

desc:
A temporary camp has been pitched here. A twenty-foot diameter area of ground
has been roughly stamped flat with a small fire pit in its centre. Litter and
refuse lie in piles beyond the inhabited areas, some of it obviously pitched
from the sleeping area. Outside of the camp, the plains are generally flat and
covered with a half-eaten layer of vegetation.
name: A Small Camp

desc:
The ground is generally flat, covered in a constant layer of grass which has
been eaten close to the ground. The animals have made crude paths which zig-zag
through the area, mostly going from one grazing patch to another. As would be
expected, large amounts of dung litter the ground, creating a fertile
environment for the grass to regrow.
name: Green Pasture Land

desc:
The ground here is almost perfectly flat, allowing any observer to see for
miles. A constant and healthy, if not overly thick, layer of grass covers the
ground as far as the eye can see. The soil here does not seem to be overly deep
and rich, the probable cause for the lack of flowers and such vegetation. A tall
ridge rises up some thirty feet in the east, providing a good view of the
surroundings.
name: Green Plains

desc:
A tall ridge is easily the most noticeable feature in the immediate area. The
majority of it is covered with scattered bushes and tough weeds, growing out of
cracks in the rock. One of the flatter sections of stone has crude paintings
drawn on it, faded with age. The slope to the east is sharp and would require an
agile climber, but the western descent is much less dangerous.
name: Ridge in the Plains

desc:
Patches of bare rock show through the grass in places, unusual in these healthy
plains of Hollin. Some stones can be found lying on top of the ground, from
fist-sized to weighing several hundred pounds. It does not appear that very many
animals make their homes here, prefering the more verdant parts of the plains. A
ridge rises high in the east, with a steep bank which offers many places for a
climber to find purchase.
name: Plains by a Ridge

desc:
Small piles of stones have been made at irregular intervals. No obvious markings
can be seen on them, so they may not have any sort of religious significance. A
healthy but short layer of grass lies on the ground, obviously eaten short by
grazing animals. There may have been flowers in the area at one time, but they
have all been eaten or trampled.
name: Fertile Pasture Land

desc:
The ground has been trampled by many, many feet. The grass, while once green,
has been beaten into the ground by a constant traffic of herds moving about the
area. Many patches of earth have been torn up by cloven hooves, creating a brown
rather than green landscape. Some rocks have been inadvertently dug up, lying on
top of the soil.
name: Trampled Grass

desc:
Slight tilts to the land cause a series of intersecting hills with gentle
slopes. A layer of thick and lush grass causes steps to bounce with extra
energy. Many patches of flowers make the plains seem less than drab, despite the
effect of continuing on for miles in every direction.
name: Fertile Pasture Land

desc:
An almost perfectly level and green land has some bright colours added by many
patches of thriving flowers. The varied types of vegatation compete to reach the
sun, with grasses and flowers growing over a foot tall, and grains higher still.
Many small trails in the vegetation attest that this area is home to a large
number of animals.
name: Fertile Plains

desc:
The land here has little slope to it, creating an almost perfectly level area
several miles square. The ground is mostly covered with a mixed layer of grass
and wild grains. The grains stand about three feet high, and grow in uneven
clusters. While the area has rocks and stones, the majority of them appear to
have been piled together long ago.
name: Vast Expanse of Green Fields

desc:
The plains here have small dips and rises, creating an uneven series of
miniature hills. Most of the ground is covered by a healthy layer of grass, but
some areas have clusters of hay growing several feet tall. Areas have seen heavy
grazing, with the vegetation cropped short. While there are stones in the area,
they seem to have been cleared from the portions which the hay grows in.
name: Fields beside a Small Hill

desc:
While the ground is mostly flat, it is difficult to see any particular thing on
the ground from a distance away. A tall layer of hay and wild grains grow
several feet tall, and obscure what may lie on the ground. The whole landscape
seems to move as breezes cause wave-like swells through the taller grains.
name: Lush Fields

desc:
The ground is mostly flat, covered in a rich and healthy layer of grass. A small
and sheltered hollow contains a small pond, a precious commodity in times of
little rain. Many patches of flowers grow around the pond, watered well. There
are many signs of animals frequenting this area.
name: Small Pond in the Plains

desc:
The ground here is mostly flat, with the occasional swell to break up the
monotony of the otherwise flat plains. A rich and fertile layer of vegetation
covers the ground, comprising a mixture of grasses, weeds, and wild grains. A
clump of bushes rests in a shallow hollow, marginally sheltered from the
elements.
name: Wide Plain

desc:
A perfectly healthy and fertile section of plains can be found here. Healthy
green grass covers the ground, interspersed with patches of multi-coloured
flowers. There is little slope to the land, creating no shelter against the
elements. The land to the east has seen heavy grazing, with piles of dung and
the grass sheared short.
name: Green Pasture Land

desc:
A short layer of wild grasses covers the flat plains, creating a layer of green
that stretches from horizon to horizon. Almost every foot of the greenery has
been eaten to within a couple of inches high. Many piles of animal dung indicate
that herds visit this area regularly. While animal tracks abound, they are so
numerous that it is impossible to tell which track came from which animal.
name: Short-Cropped Grass in the Plains

desc:
Small undulations of the ground cause a regular pattern of dips and rises, but
none of them rise to the height of a moderately-sized hill. A constant layer of
grass covers the ground, with patches of clover thriving in places. Both short
grass and scattered dung attest that this area has seen grazing recently.
name: Wide and Grassy Plains

desc:
The ground takes a dip below the surrounding countryside, creating a wide bowl
about fifteen feet deep. The slopes of the bowl are covered in a variety of
wildflowers, which make a colourful display on the lush grass. This depression
is made to look more severe than it is by the ridge to the east, which rises
well above the surrounding plains.
name: Small Dip in the Land

desc:
This small hill is some twenty feet above the flat plains, allowing a fair view
of the surroundings. Grass grows in profusion, creating a deep carpet over its
entire surface. The slope to the south descends slowly, but rises again to form
a ridge higher and much steeper than this.
name: Gentle Hill beside a Ridge

desc:
The ground here is level with most of the plains, but the presence of an almost
vertical slope to the north and a hill to the south give the illusion of being
in a depression. Rocks dot the area, and have most likely fallen from the ridge
to the north. The tough grasses growing here look slightly withered, as if they
are not receiving enough sunlight.
name: Between Two Gently Sloping Hills

desc:
The plains here are mostly flat, with a slight rolling to the gait of the land.
No rises are more than a couple of feet high, with gentle slopes down all sides.
A thick and healthy layer of grass covers the entire landscape, suggesting that
this area would be ideal for agriculture. To the east, a ridge with a moderate
slope rises above the plains.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
This land is mostly flat. It rises and falls seldom, creating good vantage
points few and far between. A constant layer of grass covers the ground, eaten
short by many animals. Bushes and shrubs grow clustered together, sheltering
each other from the wind. Old animal droppings can be seen at a glance. A herd
of some sort must frequent this area often.
name: Plains of Eregion

desc:
The ground here is composed of gentle slopes, creating a series of small rises
in the plains. A healthy layer of grass hugs the ground, giving an extra spring
to a traveller's step. Some patches of grass have been cropped short where a
herd has stopped to graze. The air is fresh and clean, blowing across the plains
for many miles without encountering the taint of civilization.
name: Grass-covered Plains

desc:
The land to the north is rocky and has little animal or plant life on it. The
land gets progressively more fertile as it travels to the south, causing much
more healthy vegetation to grow. Small changes in elevation begin in the east,
creating a series of grass-covered rises. Sections of grass have seen grazing
recently, probably from a roaming herd.
name: Plains of Eregion

desc:
A constant layer of growing grass covers this generally flat area, creating
ideal condition for animals to graze. Some sections of the vegetation have been
eaten close to the ground, attesting that herds occasionally visit this region.
Some patches of weeds and grains grow here, rising up to two feet higher than
the surrounding vegetation.
name: Green Plains

desc:
This mostly flat area is covered with a healthy layer of grass and other forms
of vegetation. There are sections of land which have had the stones cleared off
and piled together. Patches of grains still grow there, even though it appears
